situation.content:
You ask a colleague for context on a project and get a carefully edited summary — enough to not seem unhelpful, but missing the details that would actually make you effective. Or you notice that the person who always seems to know everything never quite shares the full picture unprompted.

They're not malicious. They're rational. In most organizations, information is the only currency that doesn't depreciate when you spend it — unless you give it away.

story.content:
In 1943, over 75,000 people worked at Oak Ridge, Tennessee — a secret city that didn't appear on any map. They operated enormous machines, monitored gauges, moved materials between buildings. Almost none of them knew what they were building.

This was by design. General Leslie Groves, who ran the Manhattan Project, implemented extreme compartmentalization: each worker knew only their specific task. The calutron operators — mostly young women recruited from rural Tennessee — knew they were separating something, but not what or why. When asked what they did, they'd say "I watch a dial and keep the needle in the middle." They were enriching uranium for an atomic bomb.

Groves's logic was straightforward: information shared is information leaked. In wartime, with spies actively targeting the project, restricting knowledge flow was a survival strategy. The fewer people who understood the full picture, the harder it was for the whole to be compromised.

Here's what makes this relevant beyond wartime: Groves's logic is the same logic operating in every organization that hoards information. The stakes are lower, but the mechanism is identical. Knowledge is leverage. Sharing knowledge reduces leverage. Therefore, don't share.

The project manager who keeps the roadmap vague? They're ensuring they remain the essential translator between teams. The senior engineer who documents nothing? They're making themselves irreplaceable. The executive who shares strategy only in fragments? They're maintaining the power differential that comes from being the only one who sees the whole board.

None of these people think of themselves as information hoarders. They have reasonable-sounding stories: "It's need-to-know." "I don't want to overwhelm people." "The details would just confuse them." These stories are the civilian version of Groves's compartmentalization logic — and they carry the same hidden cost.

Oak Ridge worked — they built the bomb. But the compartmentalization also meant that brilliant physicists couldn't optimize across the system, that errors in one section propagated without correction, and that the human cost of the project was invisible to the people causing it. Hoarding works for control. It fails for intelligence. The system gets dumber as it gets more controlled.

The same trade-off operates in every team: hoarding information increases individual power and decreases collective intelligence. The hoarder becomes more important and the system becomes more fragile.
story.title: The Story

contrast.content: In 1991, Linus Torvalds posted the Linux kernel source code publicly and invited strangers to improve it. By every rational calculation, this was insane — giving away your intellectual property to competitors. But the opposite happened: sharing created more power, not less. Thousands of contributors made Linux better than any closed team could. Torvalds became more influential by giving information away than Groves ever was by locking it down. The counter-intuitive truth: in complex systems, information shared compounds. Information hoarded decays.
contrast.title: The Contrast

principle.content:
Information hoarding is a rational response to environments where knowledge equals power. People don't hoard because they're selfish — they hoard because the incentive structure rewards it.

The fix isn't moral appeals ("be more transparent!"). It's redesigning incentives so that sharing information creates more power than hoarding it. When being the person who connects others is more valuable than being the person who knows more than others, hoarding stops making sense.

psychology.content:
Information hoarding activates the same neural circuits as resource scarcity. The brain treats knowledge as a finite resource — sharing feels like losing something. This is the "endowment effect" applied to information: once you possess knowledge, giving it away triggers loss aversion.

Research on organizational knowledge sharing (Davenport & Prusak, 1998) consistently shows that the #1 predictor of hoarding isn't personality — it's whether the environment rewards sharing or punishes it. In cultures where credit flows to the source ("Ahmed's analysis showed..."), sharing increases. In cultures where credit flows to the presenter ("Great presentation, Sarah"), the person upstream has every reason to keep their insights private.

There's also the "indispensability strategy": in environments with high job insecurity, making yourself the single point of failure is rational self-preservation. The person who documents everything can be replaced. The person whose knowledge lives only in their head cannot. Hoarding is often a fear response dressed as a power move.
psychology.title: Why It Happens

move.content:
1. Diagnose the incentive, not the person. When someone hoards, ask: what does this environment reward? If being the bottleneck gets you job security, visibility, or power, hoarding is rational. Change the incentive before blaming the behavior.

2. Make sharing more powerful than knowing. Create systems where the person who shares information gets credit for the downstream outcomes it enables. "Ahmed's framework helped three teams ship faster" is more career-advancing than "Ahmed knows something no one else does."

3. Reduce the cost of being replaceable. Hoarding often comes from insecurity. If documenting your knowledge makes you feel dispensable, the problem isn't your character — it's that the organization punishes replaceability. Make people safe enough to share.

4. Share first. In prisoner's-dilemma dynamics, the most effective strategy is generous tit-for-tat: share first, then mirror what comes back. If you want information to flow, model the behavior. Most hoarding loops start because everyone is waiting for someone else to go first.

5. Build information systems that make hoarding harder. When knowledge lives in shared documents, Slack channels, and wikis by default, hoarding requires active effort. Make sharing the path of least resistance and hoarding the path of most resistance.
move.title: The Move
